protocols.title: Copy-number inference and genome instability quantification
steps: ### word/document.xml Copy-number inference and genome instability quantification Copy-number profiles were derived from segmented sequencing data using ASCAT, which provides allele-specific copy-number estimates together with tumour purity and ploidy inference (Van Loo et al., 2010).

Given the high degree of aneuploidy in HGSC , copy-number alterations were defined using ploidy-normalised values, calculated as: LogR =Lo g 2 CNtotal ploidy where CNtotal denotes the ASCAT-derived total copy number ( nAraw + nBraw), and ploidy corresponds to the tumour ploidy estimate for the respective sample.
Segments with logR values greater than 1.3 were classified as amplifications, whereas segments with values below −1 were classified as deletions.
The amplification threshold approximately corresponds to copy-number states exceeding ~2.5-fold above tumour ploidy and was selected to enrich for high-level focal amplification events.
Conversely, the deletion threshold corresponds approximately to copy-number states below half the tumour ploidy and was intended to capture substantial allelic loss and deep deletion events.
Segments that did not meet these criteria were considered copy-number neutral in downstream analyses.

Fraction of Genome Altered (FGA) was calculated as the proportion of the evaluated genome affected by copy-number alterations: FGA = Σ L altered L genome where Laltered represents the cumulative genomic length of segments classified as amplified or deleted, and Lgenome denotes the total genomic length assessed.
Only segments classified as amplified or deleted according to the thresholds defined above were included in the altered fraction.
For downstream analyses, samples were ranked according to FGA values and stratified into tertiles representing low, intermediate, and high levels of genomic alteration burden.
Whole-genome doubling (WGD) was inferred from allele-specific copy-number profiles using the framework described by Zhang et al.
(2025), based on the fraction of the genome with major allele copy number ≥2 (FM2).
For each sample, the fraction was calculated as: FM2 = Σ L major CN 2 L genome where Lmajor CN≥2 denotes the cumulative genomic length of segments with major-allele copy number ≥2.

document_index.name: ASCAT_report_final.pdf
document_index.title: Comparison and Normalization of Two ASCAT
document_index.excerpt: Comparison and Normalization of Two ASCAT The aim of this project was to compare and normalize two separate ASCAT result datasets: one run with WES data, another with SNP-array data, and build combined comparison tables for easy observation of copy number alterations (CNA) in HGSC. ASCAT takes LogR (total signal intensity at a locus, roughly log2(observed/expected)) and BAF (B allele frequency) as input, and outputs per-sample tumor purity and ploidy, plus per-segment allele-specific copy numbers (nMajor, nMinor). The key technical difference between the two platforms lies in how LogR is derived: in WES it comes from read depth, whereas in SNP-array it comes from hybridization intensity. Platform-specific parameters used in ASCAT and the normalization methods also affect the final copy num

document_index.excerpt: We wanted to study the association between WES-derived genomic features, such as tumor ploidy and whole-genome doubling, and t-CyCIF-derived morphological features including cell nuclear area and nuclear eccentricity. Ploidy - tumor nuclear area association We found a positive correlation between tumor ploidy and median tumor nuclear area ( ). document_index.name: SPACE_building_blocks.docx
document_index.title: SPACE: Precision oncology of spatial immune escape mechanisms in ovarian cancer -
document_index.excerpt: SPACE: Precision oncology of spatial immune escape mechanisms in ovarian cancer - , Andreas Hainari, Venla Kaislo, Saundarya Shah, Lina Maltrovsky, Anastasia Lundgren, Essi Kahelin, Matilda Salko, Anni Virtanen, Ulla-Majia Haltia, Anniina Färkkila High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) is the most lethal subtype of epithelial ovarian cancer, due to late-stage diagnosis, high rates of recurrence, and frequent resistance to therapy. Based on the ability of genomic recombination repair, HGSC tumors are commonly stratified into two major subtypes: homologous recombination deficient (HRD) and homologous recombination proficient (HRP). HRD tumors typically contain BRCA mutations and are more responsive to platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitors. In contrast, HRP tumors lack these defici

document_index.name: README.docx
document_index.title: Responsible: Ziqi Kang
document_index.excerpt: SPACE means Precision oncology of spatial immune escape mechanisms in ovarian cancer. The aim is to uncover the spatial immune escape mechanisms in chemo-naive HGSC samples and profile the spatial landscape of the treatment-naive tumor microenvironment (TME). We will propose a special definition of niche in the tumor ecosystem - niche = physical niche + functional niche - and use the concept of “niche” as a tool to reveal immune escape mechanisms. Additionally, establishing a model to integrate multiomic datasets.

10.03.2025
Kassandra usage
https://science.bostongene.com/kassandra/tpm-description
Kassandra uses the TPM (TPM stands for transcript per million, and the sum of all TPM values is the same in all samples, such that a TPM value represents a relative expression level that, in principle, should be comparable between samples) expression of transcripts (gene isoforms) produced by Kallisto
[1]
 (
https://pachterlab.github.io/kallisto/starting
) as input. The Kallisto index file should be downloaded from the 
CGL pipeline repository
 (inputs, kallisto_hg38.idx) from the Toil project 
[2]
, whose results are hosted on UCSC 
Xena
. This index file was built based on 
GENCODE transcriptome annotation version 23
 (comprehensive gene annotation, regions ALL) and the human reference genome GRCh38 with genes from the PAR locus removed (chrY:10,000-2,781,479 and chrY:56,887,902-57,217,415). In contrast to paired-end fastq files, single-end fastq files should be processed by Kallisto with additional options -l 200 -s 15 in line with Toil.

RCh38 with genes from the PAR locus removed (chrY:10,000-2,781,479 and chrY:56,887,902-57,217,415). In contrast to paired-end fastq files, single-end fastq files should be processed by Kallisto with additional options -l 200 -s 15 in line with Toil.
Utilizing input in the form of gene TPM either produced from Kallisto output by a procedure differing from the procedure described in the Kassandra study or produced by other tools for expression quantification is 
possible but not fully recommended.
As explained in the Kassandra study, 
transcripts belonging to several groups according to their biological types or quality of annotation and reference sequence are excluded from the TPM dataframe combined from the raw Kallisto outputs
. Then, the 
sum of expression of all retained transcripts is normalized to 1,000,000
, which results in 
adjusted TPM values
. Finally, 
each gene is assigned a TPM expression value
 by summing the TPM values of its transcripts according to the GENCODE transcriptome annotation.
This procedure of 
transcript filtration and TPM renormalization cannot be performed on gene TPM data
. However, if gene TPM data is submitted to Kassandra as input, it will renormalize TPM at the gene level (by the list of genes), which may lead to incorrect deconvolution. 
Steps
: 
Get TPM values from DRAGEN pipeline 
used in iCAN platform - a bit tricky
Make sure we get the correct .sf files, there are 2, one transcript and one gene. 
Prepare yaml files like 
https://github.com/BostonGene/Kassandra/blob/main/configs/cell_types.yaml
 in order to run Kassandra
Run Kassandra analysis and check the result

11.03.2025
Spatial metrics for tCyCIF images
Basic metric
# cell types and # RCN in each image
Spatial metric - 1: Some tumor & stroma-related metrics
Tumor/stroma ratio
Tumor percentage
Tumor_stroma_interface_length: with max_dist = 50. -- This metric measures the physical contact between tumor cells and stromal cells in an image, which reflects how much interaction occurs at the boundary between these 2 cell populations. Larger length -> more interaction that can indicate the shape of the tumor or the infiltration of the tumor. 
Spatial metric - 2: Single cell type distribution metrics.
 For each cell type, calculate: 
NND: nearest neighbour distance, measure the distribution of certain cell type, are they loose or dense. 
Cluster Density: Compute the density of clusters with DBSCAN, radius = 30, and a minimum of 10 cells considered as a cluster. 
Gini Density: Gini coefficient for cell density variation, measure the inequality of a distribution. Gini density closer to 1 means a more unequal distribution of certain cell types. For example, the unequal distribution of immune cells means there might be some immune structures like tertiary lymphoid nodes or immune networks. 
Implementation of spatial point pattern analysis - very time-consuming
We need to have windows/ROIs on the image and then perform point pattern analysis! 
Ripley’s K Function: 
Assesses spatial clustering or dispersion of points over various scales. Determines if features (e.g., cells in medical images) are randomly distributed, clustered, or uniformly dispersed.
Pair Correlation Function (g-function): 
Evaluates the frequency of inter-point distances, offering insights into local spatial structures. Identifies specific scales at which clustering or regular patterns occur.

clustered, or uniformly dispersed.
Pair Correlation Function (g-function): 
Evaluates the frequency of inter-point distances, offering insights into local spatial structures. Identifies specific scales at which clustering or regular patterns occur.
Kernel Density Estimation (KDE): 
Estimates the intensity function of point patterns, highlighting areas of varying point density. Visualizes density variations, aiding in hotspot detection within images.
Spatial Autocorrelation (Moran’s I, Getis-Ord Gi):
* Measures the degree to which similar values cluster spatially. Detects hotspots or coldspots, indicating areas with significant spatial autocorrelation.

27.03.2025
New idea: Radius for RCN analysis
We were discussing the proper threshold to choose for the RCN analysis. There are 2 parameters needed to be decided: $r$ for radius and $k$ for the number of clusters. Here we set $k$ into a certain number (to simplify the situation), 1.5 to 2 times larger than the total number of cell types. 
Consider 2 extreme situations, one is 0 for radius, thus the RCNs only represent single cells. The other is unlimited large, so RCNs include the whole slide. If we calculate the the distance of clusters (many parameters can do that), we will get a curve which starts at mid, increase to the peak and then drop into 0.

y represent single cells. The other is unlimited large, so RCNs include the whole slide. If we calculate the the distance of clusters (many parameters can do that), we will get a curve which starts at mid, increase to the peak and then drop into 0.

Basically we cannot make perfect limits without ploidy data. However, normally the amplification limit when converted into corrected LogR would be somewhere between 1 and 1.5. For now I plotted and even though the number of changes is quite low we can see a drop in 1.2 thus this has been used as the limit for amplification. Over this means amplification, under gain and third cluster is no changes. These limits may change for example based on spatial information if we find something that would correlate!

Usage instruction from 
https://bitbucket.org/anthakki/prism/src/master/
: 
Step 1
: Estimate scale factors for the single-cell reference using 
prism-gain
 to correct for unequal total RNA. Do note that the absolute scale in general cannot be inferred, so the output magnitude is arbitrary and subject to change, and it is typical to enforce a canonical scaling using the 
-n
 option. This will read the expression data from the matrix 
sc_expr.tsv
, factorize it, and write the scale factors to the output file 
sc_gains.tsv
:
 prism-gain -H sc_expr.tsv -G sc_gains.tsv
Step 2
: Cluster single-cell data using 
prism-clust
 in order to find the constituent phenotypes. This will build a hierarchical clustering tree 
sc_tree.tsv
 from the expression data 
sc_expr.tsv
, and the cut the tree using 
Bayesian Information Criterion
 (
-s bic
) into an adjacency matrix suitable for 
prism-decom
 which is output into 
sc_weights.tsv
:
 prism-clust -H sc_expr.tsv -g sc_gains.tsv -T sc_tree.tsv
 prism-clust -H sc_expr.tsv -t sc_tree.tsv -s bic -W sc_weights.tsv
Step 3
: Decompose bulk expression profiles using 
prism-decom
. This will decompose each mixture expression profile from 
bulk_expr.tsv
 into the cell type specific profiles adapted for each bulk 
decom_bulk_expr.tsv
 using the single-cell reference. 
sc_gains.tsv
 are the scale factors and 
sc_weights.tsv
 the cell type adjacency matrix, as computed above, while the analysis outputs the scale factors for the bulk profiles in

for each bulk 
decom_bulk_expr.tsv
 using the single-cell reference. 
sc_gains.tsv
 are the scale factors and 
sc_weights.tsv
 the cell type adjacency matrix, as computed above, while the analysis outputs the scale factors for the bulk profiles in 
bulk_gains.tsv
, and the estimated composition of the bulk profiles in 
bulk_weights.tsv
.
 prism-decom -H bulk_expr.tsv sc_expr.tsv -g sc_gains.tsv -w sc_weights.tsv \
 -Z decom_bulk_expr.tsv -G bulk_gains.tsv -W bulk_weights.tsv 
The bulk expression matrix 
bulk_expr.tsv
 is an m-by-r matrix, where m denotes the number of genes (rows) and n the number of bulk samples (columns) and single-cell expression matrix 
sc_expr.tsv 
is an m-by-n matrix, where n denotes the number of single-cell samples. 
The rows must be matching and regardless if the labels are provided this is not checked.
The decomposed profiles 
decom_bulk_expr.tsv
 can be used e.g. for performing a cell type specific differential expression analysis instead of the raw bulk data 
bulk_expr.tsv
, e.g. using 
DESeq2
, or either the expression features or compositional features can be directly associated with clinical features. Typically, a product of the gain factors 
bulk_gains.tsv
 and the composition 
bulk_weights.tsv
 are used as scale factors in subsequent analysis.

Plan for the downstream analysis based on PRISM deconvolution
Prefer pathways over single genes
With n=74, pathway scores are more stable and interpretable. Use MSigDB Hallmark + a few custom gene sets (HRD/DDR, EMT, proliferation). Score per sample with GSVA (ssGSEA) or singscore.
Descriptive structure (unsupervised)
PCA/UMAP on pathway scores. Consensus clustering (k=2–4) to find tumor states; check stability carefully. Check associations of clusters with HRD/HRP, purity, stage, etc. Use Fisher’s test or Wilcoxon (with BH correction).
Targeted differential analysis
For HRD vs HRP or other binary groups: run limma on pathway scores (primary) and optionally on genes (secondary) with covariates (batch, purity). Report effect sizes (mean differences) with BH FDR. Focus on top ~5–10 coherent pathways.

Differential expression analysis
Prior to differential expression analysis (DEA), we refined the matrix of raw read counts by filtering out genes that were not adequately profiled. The criteria for retaining genes in the analysis were as follows: (1) the gene must have a minimum of

rior to differential expression analysis (DEA), we refined the matrix of raw read counts by filtering out genes that were not adequately profiled. The criteria for retaining genes in the analysis were as follows: (1) the gene must have a minimum of 
15 total reads
 across samples within each comparison group, and (2) the gene must have at least 
10 counts
 in at least some samples within each group. This filtering process resulted in an input matrix comprising 15,730 genes across 58 samples. For the DEA, we utilized the Python implementation of the 
DESeq2 framework pydeseq v0.4.4
. This analysis aimed to identify genes differentially expressed between the chemo-refractory and chemo-sensitive tumors, with SBS3 and refractory/sensitive status as design factors in our model. We incorporated a unique approach for determining size factors, as follows: we utilized scale factors derived for each RNA sample through the PRISM framework, multiplied by the EOC abundance, and normalized by the geometric mean across all samples.
TF and pathway activity inference
To assess the pathway activity, we employed the Python implementation of the 
DecoupleR 
framework v1.6.0 and retrieved the 
PROGENy
 model weights with decoupler.get_progeny(top = 500). The multivariate linear model method decoupler.run_mlm() was used to infer the pathways enrichment scores. In addition to pathway activity scores, we also evaluated the activity scores of transcription factors (TF). For this purpose, we retrieved the 
CollecTRI
 gene regulatory network with decoupler.get_collectri(). For the inference of enrichment scores of TF, we utilized the univariate linear model method executed via decoupler.run_ulm().

iption factors (TF). For this purpose, we retrieved the 
CollecTRI
 gene regulatory network with decoupler.get_collectri(). For the inference of enrichment scores of TF, we utilized the univariate linear model method executed via decoupler.run_ulm(). We used the gene-level statistic incorporated in the stat-value of the DESeq2 output as input for this analysis.
GSEA
To infer functional enrichment scores, Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) was conducted on the Trimmed Mean of M-values (TMM) normalized EOC expression profiles obtained using the Python package conorm v1.2.0. We utilized the Hallmark gene sets from the Molecular Signatures Database23 (MSigDB) collection for this analysis. The GSEA was performed using the gseapy Python library v1.1.4, applying the following parameters: ranking method set to 't_test,' a total of 1000 permutations for robust statistical assessment, and the inclusion criteria for gene sets were defined with a minimum of 15 genes and a maximum of 500 genes per set. We considered a pathway significantly enriched if Benjamini–Hochberg (BH)-adjusted p < 0.1.

Question: 
The key idea of my current project is “Intertumoral TME heterogeneity established before therapeutic interventions often portends diverse patient outcomes.” My study is based on a chemo-naive high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) cohort. We want to know (1) what the key TME heterogeneity is in physical and functional? (2) Link to what outcome, (3) What is the mechanism behind the link - how does TME affect clinical outcome, and (4) could it be the evidence for therapy recommendation? If my cohort only has progression yes & no or long & short PFS, the question would be simple to solve. However, HGSC has subtypes of HRD and HRP, which have different therapies and thus add complexity to the patient outcome. 
Deep search result:
TME Heterogeneity in Ovarian Cancer
Multivariate Cox Models or Propensity Score Matching (PSM)?

Preliminary vTMA result
Using a subset of 10 images. 
Feature interpretation: 
'X_centroid', 'Y_centroid' - in um. 
'Area', 'Eccentricity', - the feature of cell mask, you can ignore these. 
'Sample' - sample name
'final_label' - the cell type label
'CellID' - unique id per cell. 
'cluster_kmeans' - RCN label before merging, you can ignore this. 
'RCNs' - use RCN 6 - Pure TSI as the label for TSI. 
'distance' - the distance from immune cell to TSI. If 0 means the immune cell is located in TSI region. 
'distance_label' - if d < mean distance for that cell type, labeled as 'low'. 
If mean<= d< 500, labeled as 'mid'. 
If d >= 500 labeled as 'high'
Please mainly focus on cell type proportions and distance_label proportions. 
The aim is to ensure the TMAs can catch similar proportions of immune cells as whole slide; and can recover TSI biology (based on the distance_label). 
The setting of TMA cores: 3 TSI core, 3 tumor core per sample.

Description for ROIs selection: 
For each case, six TMA cores were selected on a representative H&E slide: three from the main tumor and three from the tumor–stroma interface (TSI). Main tumor cores were chosen from viable, tumor-rich intratumoral regions while avoiding necrosis, hemorrhage, artifacts, and non-neoplastic tissue. TSI cores were centered on morphologically well-defined tumor–stroma boundaries to intentionally capture both epithelial and stromal compartments within the same punch. When multiple eligible regions were present, spatially separated areas were preferentially selected to capture intratumoral heterogeneity. Initial ROI annotations were performed according to predefined criteria and subsequently reviewed by a pathologist.
Given your project, I would justify the design as:
•
main tumor cores estimate intratumoral epithelial biology
•
TSI cores enrich for tumor–microenvironment interaction
•

Motivation:
How do tumor-intrinsic genomic features shape the pre-established (PDS) tumor microenvironment (TME) in HGSC, relate to patient prognosis and therapeutic response?

Pre-established HGSC-TME has spatial immune architectures that can associate with prognosis.
Tumor-intrinsic CNA states and downstream transcriptional programs

Spatial multi-omics resolves functional immune & tumor states underlying new architectures and suggests therapeutic targets.

### word/document.xml
Copy-number inference and genome instability quantification
Copy-number profiles were derived from segmented sequencing data using ASCAT, which provides allele-specific copy-number estimates together with tumour purity and ploidy inference (Van Loo et al., 2010). Given the high degree of aneuploidy in HGSC
, 
copy-number alterations were defined using ploidy-normalised values, calculated as:
LogR =Lo
g
2
CNtotal
ploidy
where 
CNtotal
 denotes the ASCAT-derived total copy number ( nAraw + nBraw), and 
ploidy
 corresponds to the tumour ploidy estimate for the respective sample. Segments with logR values greater than 1.3 were classified as amplifications, whereas segments with values below −1 were classified as deletions. The amplification threshold approximately corresponds to copy-number states exceeding ~2.5-fold above tumour ploidy and was selected to enrich for high-level focal amplification events. Conversely, the deletion threshold corresponds approximately to copy-number states below half the tumour ploidy and was intended to capture substantial allelic loss and deep deletion events. Segments that did not meet these criteria were considered copy-number neutral in downstream analyses.
Fraction of Genome Altered (FGA) was calculated as the proportion of the evaluated genome affected by copy-number alterations:
FGA = 
Σ
L
altered
L
genome
where 
Laltered
 represents the cumulative genomic length of segments classified as amplified or deleted, and 
Lgenome
 denotes the total genomic length assessed. Only segments classified as amplified or deleted according to the thresholds defined above were included in the altered fraction.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 2_Methods & Experiments/Copy-number inference and genome instability quantification.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: 2_Methods & Experiments/Copy-number inference and genome instability quantification.docx
vector_chunks.text:
genomic length of segments classified as amplified or deleted, and 
Lgenome
 denotes the total genomic length assessed. Only segments classified as amplified or deleted according to the thresholds defined above were included in the altered fraction. For downstream analyses, samples were ranked according to FGA values and stratified into tertiles representing low, intermediate, and high levels of genomic alteration burden.
Whole-genome doubling (WGD) was inferred from allele-specific copy-number profiles using the framework described by Zhang et al. (2025), based on the fraction of the genome with major allele copy number ≥2 (FM2). For each sample, the fraction was calculated as:
FM2 = 
Σ
L
major CN >2
L
genome
where 
Lmajor CN≥2
 denotes the cumulative genomic length of segments with major-allele copy number ≥2. Samples with FM2 values exceeding 0.5 were classified as WGD-positive according to the original framework. FM2 values were clustered using k-means (
k
 = 3) and ordered by increasing genome-doubling burden (WGD0–WGD2). 
To evaluate loss of heterozygosity 
(LOH),
 B-allele frequency (BAF) values at single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) loci were extracted from the ASCAT output. To quantify the extent of allelic imbalance, BAF values were transformed into a continuous, normalised LOH metric (Lavikka et al., 2024) utilising the following equation:
LOH =abs(BAF-0.5)
On this continuous scale, a value of 0 indicates complete heterozygosity (balanced alleles), whereas a value of 1 represents a total loss of heterozygosity.
Associations between WGD status and FGA strata were assessed using Fisher’s exact test, with odds ratios and 95% confidence interval
s reported.

Comparison and Normalization of Two ASCAT
Cohorts
Anni Lindfors
21.4.2026


1. Background and Goal
The aim of this project was to compare and normalize two separate ASCAT result datasets: one
run with WES data, another with SNP-array data, and build combined comparison tables for
easy observation of copy number alterations (CNA) in HGSC.


ASCAT takes LogR (total signal intensity at a locus, roughly log2(observed/expected)) and BAF
(B allele frequency) as input, and outputs per-sample tumor purity and ploidy, plus per-segment
allele-specific copy numbers (nMajor, nMinor). The key technical difference between the two
platforms lies in how LogR is derived: in WES it comes from read depth, whereas in SNP-array
it comes from hybridization intensity. Platform-specific parameters used in ASCAT and the
normalization methods also affect the final copy number estimates, creating platform-specific
biases. All work in this project was done on post-ASCAT output, with the goal of making the two
result sets more comparable.


2. The Datasets
The WES cohort has 191 unique patient IDs with complete CNV result files (segments, purity,
ploidy, LogR, LOH, gene annotations). The SNP-array cohort initially had 120 samples from re-
runs between 2020–2024, with only the core ASCAT output available.


Both datasets were unified: column names standardized, "chr" prefixes added, sorted by
position, total CN (nMajor + nMinor) computed. Basic QC removed NAs and zero-length
segments. After this, WES had 4,231,842 observations and SNP 9 255.
For SNP-array, FFPE samples that had MAPD above 0.35 were removed (31 samples) as they
introduce systematic artifacts. This brought the cohort down to 64 samples and 6 714
observations.

ments. After this, WES had 4,231,842 observations and SNP 9 255.
For SNP-array, FFPE samples that had MAPD above 0.35 were removed (31 samples) as they
introduce systematic artifacts. This brought the cohort down to 64 samples and 6 714
observations. A purity threshold of 0.3 was tested but had no real effect so wasn’t applied. WES
copy numbers were capped at nMajor = 30 (total CN ≤ 50), since values above this are almost
certainly artefacts in HGSC. Separate cohorts shared 32 same patient IDs.


Original data can be found at:
WES: smb://group3.ad.helsinki.fi/h30492/space_tcycif1/export_ican_WES/
SNP: smb://dept3.ad.helsinki.fi/ltdk_farkkila\Projects\SNP-arrays_results\
Raw_and_intermediante_files_fernando\Oncosys_and_others. Only samples from combined
2020–2024 folder, which includes most updated ASCAT runs and re-runs.
3. Initial Visualization
As a first look, copy number distributions were compared at four HGSC-relevant genes
(CCNE1, CDKN2A, MECOM, MYC) using violin + boxplots, with lines connecting five randomly
sampled matched pairs. Two versions: raw SNP and FFPE-cleaned SNP.


Figure 1. WES (pink) vs. SNP-array (blue) copy number at four HGSC genes with SNP-array containing FFPE
samples. Lines connect 5 random matched sample pairs.


Figure 2. WES (pink) vs. SNP-array (blue) copy number at four HGSC genes with cleaned SNP-array data.
Lines connect 5 random matched sample pairs.
Medians are broadly similar between platforms.


4. Building the Comparison Tables
Two types of genome-wide comparison tables were built for easy inspection and comparison of
the cohorts.

4.1 Raw Comparison Table
Copy number information was aggregated into 1 Mb bins. For each bin, length-weighted
averages were computed per sample and then summarized across the cohort (mean, median,
MAD).

easy inspection and comparison of
the cohorts.

4.1 Raw Comparison Table
Copy number information was aggregated into 1 Mb bins. For each bin, length-weighted
averages were computed per sample and then summarized across the cohort (mean, median,
MAD). WES and SNP columns are kept separate, plus a combined average was added.


Figure 3. Raw comparison: genome-wide average total CN profiles (left) and per-bin scatter (right). WES (red)
vs. SNP-array including FFPE samples (blue).


Figure 4. Same comparison after removal of FFPE samples. Average copy number profiles align better across
the genome.
Two issues were identified: (1) platform disagreement on chrX, where SNP-array gives
systematically higher CN than WES; (2) signal differences in highly amplified regions. Although,
the second issue was partly solved by restricting the SNP-ASCAT results included to 2020-2024
re-run results. Additionally, FFPE removal cleaned up poor quality samples from the SNP-array
dataset, and the cleaned version aligned better with the WES CN profile. Therefore, only the
SNP-cleaned dataset was used to construct the final combined ASCAT table.

4.2 G-Score Profiles
The second table follows GISTIC (Genomic Identification of Significant Targets in Cancer) logic:
instead of raw CN, "G-scores" represent the average gain/loss amplitude with recurrence
information. Each sample contributes once per bin, then scores are averaged across the cohort.
A separate frequency score captures recurrence. G-score profiles were computed at different
bin sizes (1 Mb, 5 Mb, 500 kb) and at gene level. All bin sizes showed similar trends.


Specifics on this can be found within ASCAT-R-documt.html file (knitted R markdown file with all
code available) at:

G-score profiles were computed at different
bin sizes (1 Mb, 5 Mb, 500 kb) and at gene level. All bin sizes showed similar trends.


Specifics on this can be found within ASCAT-R-documt.html file (knitted R markdown file with all
code available) at:
smb://dept3.ad.helsinki.fi/ltdk_farkkila/Projects/12-SPACE/CNV_correction_Anni


5. QC: Extreme CN Calls in WES
The extreme WES CN values were investigated: purity, ploidy, segment lengths, LogR profiles,
and known HGSC amplification loci were all checked. No single clear cause was found, but the
highest calls consistently came from the same patients. A likely contributor is purity estimation:
a slight underestimation of purity inflates already high CN estimates many-fold. This is a known
WES limitation at high-amplification loci.


6. Normalization: X Chromosome
The chrX discrepancy clearly comes from platform-specific biases, as the overall CN agreement
across autosomes is good. Several underlying issues can be contributers: on the WES side, the
condensed Xi and distinct GC profile can cause capture biases that lead to lower read depth
and LogR. On the SNP-array side, probe intensity chemistry tends to give higher baseline
intensity on chrX, leading to inflated LogR and higher CN estimates.


Based on published HGSC data, X chromosome gains are more common than losses, but CN
levels should not approach the most highly amplified autosomal regions. This suggests WES
calls are probably closer to the truth, though possibly slightly low, which should be kept in mind
when interpreting the results.
Figure 6. Cohort-average CN per chromosome (top) and ploidy per chromosome (bottom). SNP-array (blue)
has a clear spike at chrX vs. WES (orange). Ploidy is flat as expected, so the spike is a platform effect, not a
ploidy artifact.

ing the results.
Figure 6. Cohort-average CN per chromosome (top) and ploidy per chromosome (bottom). SNP-array (blue)
has a clear spike at chrX vs. WES (orange). Ploidy is flat as expected, so the spike is a platform effect, not a
ploidy artifact.

As a solution, an autosomal-anchored correction factor was computed to scale SNP-based chrX
CN calls to WES levels. The assumption is that biological effects change autosomes and chrX
together, while platform bias affects chrX disproportionately, and WES is the more reliable
reference.
correction_factor = (WES chrX/autosome ratio) / (SNP chrX/autosome ratio) SNP chrX CN
corrected = SNP chrX CN × correction_factor [factor: 0.696]


Figure 7. After applying the correction factor, SNP chrX (solid blue) follows the WES profile (red) well. The
difference track is near-zero across autosomes and much reduced on chrX.
7. Normalization: High-Amplification Regions
SNP-array is the traditional gold standard for CNV, with smooth BAF and low noise, and ASCAT
was originally built for it. However, hybridization intensity can saturate at extreme amplification,
meaning very high CN values may be underestimated.
WES read depth should scale linearly with copy number in theory, but noise and purity
estimation errors can push calls very high at already amplified loci.
The truth is probably somewhere in between: WES gives the right direction, but extremes
should be treated with caution. Two steps were taken: WES peaks were capped at total CN =
50, and data were transformed to log2 space.
With the cleaned SNP-array data, the ASCAT cohorts agree well across the genome. No heavy
normalization was needed beyond the steps described above.


8. Solution: Log2 Space + X Chromosome Anchoring
Log2 transformation is standard for ratio-based genomic data.

eaned SNP-array data, the ASCAT cohorts agree well across the genome. No heavy
normalization was needed beyond the steps described above.


8. Solution: Log2 Space + X Chromosome Anchoring
Log2 transformation is standard for ratio-based genomic data. Benefits include more symmetry
between gains and losses, multiplicative biases become additive constants that are easier to
correct, amplification peaks are compressed, and it’s closer to the natural domain of the data
(LogR). The X chromosome correction is applied in log2 space using the same autosomal-
anchoring principle.
Two metrics were computed: log2(total CN) and log2(CN/ploidy).


The ploidy-normalized version centers at 0 for ploidy-neutral state, which is easy to interpret as
relative gain or loss. Final tables built at 1 Mb resolution with length-weighted averaging per
sample, then cohort average. Bins with low sample coverage were excluded. Additionally, one
value was replaced with cohort average log2 values, since it repeatitevily resulted in clear
artifact outlier (snp: bin endpos = 46679698 at chr 21).


Figure 8. Final log2-space scatter: log2(total CN) (left) and log2(CN/ploidy) (right), WES vs. SNP. ChrX bins in
red. Both align tightly along the 1:1 line, confirming the cohorts are comparable.
9. Results and Summary
The two ASCAT cohorts (WES with 191 samples and SNP-array with 56 samples after QC)
show good agreement in baseline copy number profiles across autosomes. Two platform biases
were found and corrected:
1. chrX: SNP-array overestimates chrX CN due to probe chemistry. Fixed with autosomal-
anchored correction factor in linear and log2 spaces.
2. High-amplification peaks: Handled by capping at total CN = 50 and working in log2 space.

Matias experiments summary:
With NKI TMAs, I trained a PDS vs IDS classifier. I got a good performance using DNA1, CK7 and Vimenting channels as the input to a multiple instance learning (MIL) model. I also tested using more than three channels, and the performance was worse than with the previously mentioned 3 channels (I tried adding more channels to that combo and tried different ones, but the results were always worse). In the architecture of that model, I tested using a model trained from scratch or using pretrained embeddings from UNI and Gigapath as input. UNI performed the best, although both gigapath and model from scratch didn't have that much of a difference in performance (0.82 vs 0.80 F1 score in the test set). For testing more than 3 channels, I tried using the model trained from scratch and I also tested getting pre-trained embeddings (UNI and Gigapath) for different three channels combinations and then feeding that way more than 3 channels to the MIL model. Still, the results were worse than with just passing three channels (we should also consider the limited dataset size for assessing these results).
Currently, I am trying to run models on the tiles (I generated the tiles detecting tissue and removing background) of WSI from NKI images (22 images), with a different task, which is to predict long and short survival. I tried running model from scratch, and also using UNI preprocessed embedding for tiles. I had several issues in terms of memory to load the data for all tiles, and time to process the model exceeds the time I am getting for a CSC job in Mahti. I need to rethink the strategy.

Regarding deep-learning-driven ROI selection
The current workflow is using 1 foundational model to generate feature vectors from H&E datasets (TCGA and SPACE) -> Design downstream task (classification, short/long PFS) -> If the downstream task is performing well (high accuracy) -> it is convincing that the feature vectors (and the attention map) can be used to 
represent clinical information
 and perform ROIs selection. 
Problem
: if the downstream task is still not performing well after training on TCGA, the ROIs are not convincing to be used for spatial transcriptomic sequencing. Note that the dataset we have (including both TCGA and SPACE) might be not large enough for training. 
Olavi suggested plan B: Use multiple foundational models to generate feature vectors and visualize attention maps -> choose overlapping high attention regions (high confidence) as ROIs. No downstream task in this case. 
Problem
: We need to find a way to 
prove that high-confidence ROIs also have clinical meanings

Ongoing steps
Conclusions:
• Recurrent cellular neighbourhoods (RCNs) are enriched in different clinical groups
 The overall goal of SPACE project is to find spatial immune escape mechanisms in ovarian
 cancer.
• While identify features from spatial TME, we need to define what is a niche (physical +
 functional, with global features).
• We are in the step of quantifying physically TME niches based on tCyCIF data, meanwhile
 testing procedures of ROIs selection for the spatial transcriptomic.
• Still need to find a proper model to combine multi-level features -> clinical label + survival
 model could be a start.

Take home message:
• With a 17-marker panel we successfully phenotyped ~60M cells from 85 chemo-naive HGSC
 patients.
• Recurrent cellular neighbourhoods (RCNs) catches cross-sample consistency, and show
 niches enrichment among di erent clinical groups.
• Calculate tissue domains per sample can help catch per-sample heterogeneity, and can be
 described as a continuum.
• For NK and Treg (scattered cell types), we need point pattern analysis, not RCNs or Domains
 analysis.

• Current goal is to guide ROIs selection for the Xenium experiment!

 functions

 among patches

Results
We wanted to study the association between WES-derived genomic features, such as tumor ploidy and whole-genome doubling, and t-CyCIF-derived morphological features including cell nuclear area and nuclear eccentricity.
Ploidy - tumor nuclear area association
We found a positive correlation between tumor ploidy and median tumor nuclear area (
Fig. X
). This association was observed using both FACETS-derived and ASCAT-derived ploidy estimates 
(ASCAT: ρ = x, 95% CI [x,x], p = x ; FACETS: ρ = x, 95% CI [x,x], p = x) (ASCAT: r = x, 95% CI [x,x], p = x ; FACETS: r = x, 95% CI [x,x], p = x)
The consistency of the results across two independent ploidy estimation methods supports a robust association between tumor ploidy and nuclear size. For validation, we tested the association between tumor ploidy and other cell phenotypes and no significant correlation was found. 
Methods
Tumor ploidy was estimated using ASCAT and FACETS from whole-exome sequencing data.
Tumor cell nuclei were segmented from t-CyCIF images using nuclear masks expanded by 3 pixels 
(
x
μm).
For each sample, median tumor nuclear area was calculated across all tumor cells.

SPACE: Precision oncology of spatial immune escape mechanisms in ovarian cancer -> 
Title will be updated. 
Ziqi Kang
, Andreas Hainari, Venla Kaislo, Saundarya Shah, Lina Maltrovsky, Anastasia Lundgren, Essi Kahelin, Matilda Salko, Anni Virtanen, Ulla-Majia Haltia, Anniina Färkkila
Abstract
I
ntroduction
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) is the most lethal subtype of epithelial ovarian cancer, due to late-stage diagnosis, high rates of recurrence, and frequent resistance to therapy. Based on the ability of genomic recombination repair, HGSC tumors are commonly stratified into two major subtypes: homologous recombination deficient (HRD) and homologous recombination proficient (HRP). HRD tumors typically contain BRCA mutations and are more responsive to platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitors. In contrast, HRP tumors lack these deficiencies and often show resistance to PARP inhibitor treatment. Emerging evidence indicates that genomic instability and the resulting genomic damage may shape the tumor microenvironment (TME), influencing spatial tissue architecture and modulating immune cell behavior. Specifically, damaged or stressed tumor cells may evade immune surveillance through altered cytokine signaling, immune exclusion, or recruitment of immunosuppressive cell populations.
“Intertumoral TME heterogeneity established before therapeutic interventions often portends diverse patient outcomes.”
 To investigate how genomic background interacts with immune escape mechanisms, we analyzed chemo-naive HGSC samples stratified by clinical outcome—progression versus progression-free status. Within each outcome group, we compared the TME features of HRD and HRP tumors.

genomic background interacts with immune escape mechanisms, we analyzed chemo-naive HGSC samples stratified by clinical outcome—progression versus progression-free status. Within each outcome group, we compared the TME features of HRD and HRP tumors. This approach allowed us to assess how immune and spatial TME characteristics differ between HRD and HRP tumors under similar clinical conditions. Our goal was to identify subtype-specific immune escape strategies and spatial TME patterns that may underlie differential patient outcomes and provide a rationale for tailored therapeutic interventions.
[Then more about CNA mutation and fine-grained patient subtypes -> genetic drivers?] 
We incorporate multi-omics profiling using whole exome sequencing (WES), bulk RNA sequencing, high-dimensional whole-section tissue imaging (t-CycIF), and spatial transcriptomics from 119 (80+39) patient samples capturing over 58 million single cells and XX spatial transcriptomic domains captured by molecular profiling. By combining genomic, single-cell, and spatial data from patient-matched HGSC samples collected from chemo-naive patients, we show how the spatially cell-cell interaction neighborhoods and transcriptional programs varies due to genetic drivers, resulting….

To investigate the effects of genetic drivers on the TME in the setting of patients unaffected by therapy, we collected a series of 89 chemo-naive distinct tumor samples from 80 patients with high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC), enrolled in the Oncosys-OVA prospective observational trial (
NCT06117384
). All tumor tissue profiles were obtained from primary debulking surgery, obtained mainly from metastatic omental sites (n = 62), otherwise from the adnexa (ovaries/fallopian tube, n = 27). Detailed clinical annotations, including molecular features (OvaHRDscore), treatments (for example, PARP inhibitor) as well as surgical and survival outcomes were collected (Supplementary Table X). The tumor samples underwent multi-omics profiling with whole-exome sequencing (WES), bulk RNA-seq, tissue-based cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CyCIF) imaging, and spatial transcriptomics (Figure 1X). We obtained DNA sequencing data spanning a xx-gene panel, focused on single-nucleotide variations, somatic CNAs, chromosomal rearrangements, and tumor mutational burden (TMB), providing a basis to link tissue structure and genetic aberrations. The WES analysis results were summarised in integrative Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) reports.

riations, somatic CNAs, chromosomal rearrangements, and tumor mutational burden (TMB), providing a basis to link tissue structure and genetic aberrations. The WES analysis results were summarised in integrative Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) reports. 
To characterize the cellular landscape of these chemo-naive tumors, we integrated data across platforms. We applied whole-slide t-CycIF imaging to profile the spatial architecture of the single-cell TME, and successfully segmented and quantified 58,561,942 cells across 79 tumor samples (Fig. SX). We phenotyped 10 cell types using open-source packages Tribus and SCIMAP, including tumor cells, stromal cells, cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAF), B cells, CD4⁺ T cells, CD8⁺ T cells, regulatory T cells (Tregs), NK cells, dendritic cells (DCs), and macrophages. In parallel, we inferred fine-grained cell type proportions from bulk RNA-seq data using Kassandra, and inferred the expression matrix of tumor cells using PRISM. Across matched tumor sites and patients, estimated cell type proportions were consistent between t-CyCIF and bulk RNA-seq. The proportion of tumor cells were consistent with tumor purity estimation on WES data by ASCAT. 
Initial analyses of the data reveal heterogeneous tumor tissue compositions across patients. At the level of major cell types, we observed statistically significant trends in line with previous studies toward an increase of CAF and Treg, while a decrease of DCs and macrophage cells in pre-treatment metastatic tumors. Similarly consistent with previous studies, HRD samples have a significantly higher proportion of CD8T cells compared with HRP samples, but have a lower proportion of CD4T cells.

ecrease of DCs and macrophage cells in pre-treatment metastatic tumors. Similarly consistent with previous studies, HRD samples have a significantly higher proportion of CD8T cells compared with HRP samples, but have a lower proportion of CD4T cells. These features, established before any therapeutic intervention, may reflect inherent immunological differences shaped by genomic background and underlie divergent clinical outcomes. To explore this further, we compared HRD and HRP tumors within clinically defined progression and progression-free groups. The same trend in immune composition—higher CD8⁺ and lower CD4⁺ in HRD versus HRP—was observed in the progression-free subgroup but was absent in the progression subgroup. This suggests that tumors from patients who progress may diverge from the canonical immune phenotypes of HRD and HRP subtypes, resulting in a diminished difference in CD4⁺ and CD8⁺ T cell composition between the two groups. These atypical immune landscapes in progressing tumors may reflect biological selection pressures or alternative immune escape routes. To investigate structural features of the immune microenvironment, we next performed spatial analyses of TME organization.
While compositional shifts suggest underlying differences, they cannot further explain how immune cells are spatially organized — prompting spatial analyses of the TME.

This sets up the idea that spatial neighborhoods differ by genomic subtype beyond simple cell fractions.
To systematically analyze the spatial distribution of cell communities within the HGSC TME, we annotated 13 distinct recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs) by clustering the cellular neighborhood matrix with the SCIMAP package. The identified RCNs included one tumor cell-dominated neighborhood (RCN1), three stromal neighborhoods (RCN2, RCN8, RCN9), six immune-rich neighborhoods, and three neighborhoods localized at the tumor–stroma interface (TSI)—RCN6, RCN10, and RCN12—each containing mixed populations of tumor, stromal, and immune cells. To quantify the cellular heterogeneity within each neighborhood, we computed Rao’s quadratic entropy based on cell type proportions. RCN13 has the highest diversity (0.842) composed of tumor cells, stromal cells, and nearly all major immune cell types, with particularly high representation of CD4⁺ T cells. The TSI-related RCNs (RCN6, 10, and 12) showed moderately high diversity scores (~0.7), reflecting their mixed cellular composition. In contrast, RCN1—dominated by tumor cells—had the lowest diversity score (0.108), consistent with a spatially homogeneous, immune-excluded tumor niche. 
We then explored whether the RCNs based on spatial TME neighborhood compositions was driven by genomic backgrounds.

ominated by tumor cells—had the lowest diversity score (0.108), consistent with a spatially homogeneous, immune-excluded tumor niche. 
We then explored whether the RCNs based on spatial TME neighborhood compositions was driven by genomic backgrounds. Comparing the enrichment of RCNs across HRD and HRP groups, we found that RCN13, RCN3 and RCN9 are significantly enriched in HRP groups with RCN13 showing the largest log₂ fold change. RCN13’s high cellular diversity—marked by broad immune infiltration and abundant CD4⁺ T cells—suggests that HRP tumors more frequently support immunologically complex spatial niches. RCN3 and RCN9 represent the stroma-resident immune cell and CAF-enriched stromal–tumor interfaces. Together, the results highlight that the spatial architecture of the tumor microenvironment also differs between HRD and HRP tumors.
While RCN analysis captures spatial patterns that are consistent across tissues, it does not resolve patient-specific or fine-grained spatial features. To address this, we applied spatial point-level analysis using our in-house package, SPACEstat, focusing on immune infiltration patterns and the spatial positioning of immune cells relative to the tumor–stroma interface (TSI) in individual HGSC samples. Specifically, we quantified the distribution of cell distances to the TSI and calculated an immune infiltration index, defined as the ratio of immune cell density in tumor regions to that in stromal regions (see Methods). Comparing HRD and HRP tumors, we found that HRD samples showed significantly lower densities of CD4⁺ T cells in both tumor and stromal compartments, but higher densities of dendritic cells (DCs) and CD8⁺ T cells within tumor regions.

s (see Methods). Comparing HRD and HRP tumors, we found that HRD samples showed significantly lower densities of CD4⁺ T cells in both tumor and stromal compartments, but higher densities of dendritic cells (DCs) and CD8⁺ T cells within tumor regions. In terms of spatial positioning, HRP tumors tended to show higher immune cell proximity to the TSI across immune cell types, although these differences did not reach statistical significance.

We then performed subgroup comparisons based on clinical progression status. Among progression samples, B cells, CD4⁺ T cells, and CD8⁺ T cells were significantly closer to the TSI in HRD tumors compared to HRP tumors, both when measured globally and from the stromal side.

oup comparisons based on clinical progression status. Among progression samples, B cells, CD4⁺ T cells, and CD8⁺ T cells were significantly closer to the TSI in HRD tumors compared to HRP tumors, both when measured globally and from the stromal side. Within tumor regions, only B cells showed significantly higher proximity to the TSI in HRD tumors. B cells also had a higher immune infiltration index in HRD samples, despite exhibiting lower stromal densities. CD8⁺ T cells similarly showed higher infiltration indices in HRD tumors. In the progression-free subgroup, the trends were generally reversed. Most immune cell types—except NK cells—exhibited greater TSI proximity in HRP tumors compared to HRD tumors. B cells in HRD tumors also had lower infiltration indices and higher stromal densities relative to HRP tumors. For CD4⁺ T cells, both tumor and stromal densities, as well as the infiltration index, were significantly lower in HRD tumors. CD8⁺ T cells, however, showed higher densities in both tumor and stroma in HRD tumors, consistent with earlier observations based on cellular proportions. These comparisons indicate that the spatial distribution of immune cells relative to the tumor–stroma interface differs between HRD and HRP tumors, and that these differences vary between progression and non-progression groups. 
To further assess whether these spatial features distinguish clinical outcomes within each genomic background, we next compared spatial metrics between progression and non-progression samples separately in HRD and HRP groups. We examined immune spatial metrics within each genomic background to evaluate associations with clinical outcome.

h genomic background, we next compared spatial metrics between progression and non-progression samples separately in HRD and HRP groups. We examined immune spatial metrics within each genomic background to evaluate associations with clinical outcome. Among HRP tumors, non-progression samples exhibited significantly higher TSI proximity across all immune cell types, along with higher B cell infiltration indices and increased CD4⁺ T cell density in the tumor region. By contrast, HRD tumors showed no statistically significant differences in immune proximity, infiltration index, or immune cell densities between progression and non-progression groups, with the exception of a slight reduction in B cell infiltration index in non-progression samples. These findings indicate that HRD and HRP tumors exhibit different patterns of immune spatial organization in the treatment-naive setting, with greater variation across clinical progression groups in HRP tumors. The relatively uniform features observed in HRD tumors may indicate that additional spatial dimensions beyond TSI proximity and infiltration are needed to capture biologically relevant heterogeneity. We next examined higher-order spatial structures to further dissect immune cell organization in the treatment-naive TME.
Immune positioning relative to the TSI further stratified outcomes in HRP but not HRD tumors. These results highlight the importance of higher-order spatial architecture, motivating us to move beyond neighborhoods and test whether coordinated immune structures underlie these subtype-specific patterns.

To profile higher-order coordination among immune lineages, we modeled spatial networks of lymphoid (B, CD4⁺, CD8⁺, NK, Treg) and myeloid (macrophage, DC) cells across whole-slide images. Overlapping lymphoid and myeloid network components formed immune interaction events—referred to as immune hubs—representing locally coordinated immune aggregates.
Across all samples, immune hubs varied substantially in size, cellular composition, and spatial density. We defined immune interaction events (“immune hubs”) by overlapping lymphoid (B, CD4⁺, CD8⁺, NK, Treg) and myeloid (DC, macrophage) network components (edge ≤35 µm, inter-component distance <10 µm, ≥30 cells), then summarized each hub’s size, composition, and centroid location.

n events (“immune hubs”) by overlapping lymphoid (B, CD4⁺, CD8⁺, NK, Treg) and myeloid (DC, macrophage) network components (edge ≤35 µm, inter-component distance <10 µm, ≥30 cells), then summarized each hub’s size, composition, and centroid location. Using hub centroids, we applied ecological point-pattern statistics (Ripley’s L(r) and the pair-correlation function g(r)) and derived curve features (e.g., max_l_obs, r_maxL, r_decayL, slope_at_origin, max_pcf, r_maxg, r_decayg, mean_pcf_short, mean_pcf_mid) to cluster patients by spatial hub organization. N (8) clusters were generated in total. 
Preliminary analyses revealed that a subgroup of HRP patients exhibited very few immune interaction events, suggesting sparse lymphoid–myeloid coordination. Notably, these HRP tumors also tended to belong to the progression group, hinting that disrupted immune interaction architecture may underlie adverse outcomes. This observation motivates subsequent testing of the association between hub-based spatial features and clinical endpoints such as PFS and OS. We verified that HRP tumors with the fewest immune interaction events had the poorest progression-free outcomes, suggesting that loss of coordinated lymphoid–myeloid proximity may underlie immune dysfunction in these tumors.
Collectively, these results identify immune hubs as emergent spatial units of immune cooperation, linking genomic subtype and clinical outcome through differences in local immune coordination. We next investigated whether such spatial heterogeneity is further shaped by underlying genomic instability.

Whole-exome profiling identified recurrent copy-number amplifications affecting CCNE (n = 12), MYC (n = 18), and MECOM (n = 7). These genes were selected for further analysis owing to their known oncogenic roles and distribution across distinct chromosomes. Ongoing refinement using GISTIC2 peak calling and COSMIC annotation will improve the resolution of amplification hotspots, and integration with normalized RNA-seq expression data will verify that amplified loci correspond to high gene expression levels. 
Integrating spatial features and CNA labels revealed that copy number alterations (CNAs) correspond to distinct spatial architectures within the HGSC microenvironment.

n data will verify that amplified loci correspond to high gene expression levels. 
Integrating spatial features and CNA labels revealed that copy number alterations (CNAs) correspond to distinct spatial architectures within the HGSC microenvironment. To further stratify the HRP cohort, we compared CCNE1-amplified and CCNE1-non-amplified tumors and revealed opposite trends in spatial features, both immune infiltration and immune proximity to TSI. In general, HRP samples with amplifications tended to have less immune infiltration and lower immune proximity to TSI, consistent with a more disorganized or immune-excluded microenvironment. Noticeably, MYC and MECOM mainly affect immune density or immune infiltration, while CCNE1 also affects the immune distribution and distance to the TSI. 
These results point to a link between genomic instability and spatial disorganization. We will perform formal correlation analyses once CNA validation and integration with RNA-seq expression data are complete. 
Together, these observations support a model in which genomic instability promotes spatial chaos within the TME, disrupting immune coordination and shaping differential clinical trajectories. To functionally contextualize these spatial and genomic patterns, we next characterized the transcriptional programs underpinning distinct immune and stromal niches using spatial transcriptomics.

Recurrent cellular neighborhood analysis
Recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs) were computed using the SCIMAP package in Python, by first calculating the fractions of neighboring cell types for each centering cell using _tl.spatial_count_ with a r=300px (1px = 0.325µm), and the resulting neighborhood matrix was then clustered using _tl.spatial_cluster_ using kmeans clustering with k=20.

alculating the fractions of neighboring cell types for each centering cell using _tl.spatial_count_ with a r=300px (1px = 0.325µm), and the resulting neighborhood matrix was then clustered using _tl.spatial_cluster_ using kmeans clustering with k=20. 300 pixel radius was chosen to find large scale cellular structures from the spatial data, as the radius encompasses around 10-12 cells in length. The identity of each cluster was defined by the proportions of cell types composing it. 
RCNs enrichment analysis
The scale of cell-cell distance
We checked the distribution of the cell-cell distance scale with SPACEstat. To characterize spatial relationships between cell types, we computed nearest neighbor distance (NND) distributions for all pairwise combinations of cell types across each image. For a given cell type A, we identified the shortest Euclidean distance from each cell of type A to the nearest cell of type B. These distances were calculated in both directions (A to B and B to A), and the results were combined to represent the pairwise distribution. When analyzing a single cell type (e.g., A to A), the distance to the second-closest neighbor was used to avoid self-matching. All distances exceeding a user-defined threshold (if specified) were excluded.
Letting 
d
 represent the Euclidean distance between cell centroids, the nearest neighbor distance is defined as: 
$d = \min_j \| \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \|,$ where $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_j$ are the coordinates of cells from the respective types. The distributions were computed separately for each image and then aggregated. 
Immune-TSI study
Immune cells were spatially classified as tumor-resident or stroma-resident based on their local microenvironment.

es of cells from the respective types. The distributions were computed separately for each image and then aggregated. 
Immune-TSI study
Immune cells were spatially classified as tumor-resident or stroma-resident based on their local microenvironment. For each immune cell, we identified all neighboring cells within a radius of certain value. If >50% of these neighboring cells were annotated as tumor cells, the immune cell was considered tumor-resident; otherwise, it was classified as stroma-resident. This analysis was performed separately for each sample to preserve spatial heterogeneity. Cells without any neighbors within the radius were labeled as “undefined”. The radius is set as 80µm by checking the peak of distance between cancer and immune cells. 
Immune network analysis
For a set of points $P = \{p_1, p_2, ..., p_n\}$ in a plane, the Delaunay triangulation $DT(P)$ creates a triangulation such that no point in $P$ is inside the circumcircle of any triangle in $DT(P)$. For each network, the function finds connected components:
* $CC(L) = \{L_1, L_2, ..., L_p\}$ where each $L_i$ is a connected subgraph
* $CC(M) = \{M_1, M_2, ..., M_q\}$ where each $M_j$ is a connected subgraph
For each component, the function calculates its centroid. For example, in lymphoid component $L_i$ with cells at positions $\{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), ..., (x_n, y_n)\}$: $C_{L_i} = (\frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}x_j, \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}y_j)$.
A bipartite graph $G_{proximity}$ is constructed where:
* Nodes represent lymphoid and myeloid components
* Edges connect components whose centroids are within the proximity threshold

= (\frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}x_j, \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}y_j)$.
A bipartite graph $G_{proximity}$ is constructed where:
* Nodes represent lymphoid and myeloid components
* Edges connect components whose centroids are within the proximity threshold
For centroids $C_{L_i}$ and $C_{M_j}$, an edge exists if: $\|C_{L_i} - C_{M_j}\| \leq \text{proximity/threshold}$, where $\|C_{L_i} - C_{M_j}\|$ is the Euclidean distance: $\|C_{L_i} - C_{M_j}\| = \sqrt{(C_{L_i,x} - C_{M_j,x})^2 + (C_{L_i,y} - C_{M_j,y})^2}$.
Immune hubs are identified as connected components in $G_{proximity}$: $\text{Hubs} = CC(G_{proximity})$. Each hub contains at least one lymphoid component and one myeloid component.
For each hub $H$:
* Hub size is calculated as $\text{size}(H) = \sum_{v \in H} \text{size}(v)$.
* Lymphoid-myeloid ratio: $\text{ratio}(H) = \frac{\sum_{v \in H, \text{type}(v)=\text{lymphoid}} \text{size}(v)}{\max(1, \sum_{v \in H, \text{type}(v)=\text{myeloid}} \text{size}(v))}$.
If min_hub_size is specified, only hubs where $\text{size}(H) \geq \text{min hub size}$ are retained.
Clinical analysis - for Venla 
Cox model etc. 
Survival analyses were performed using the R packages survival and survminer. We modeled overall survival by using Kaplan-Meier estimates from survfit with 95% confidence intervals and log-rank p-values from ggsurvplot. For the Cox proportional hazard model, we used the variables that were selected in the variable selection pipeline. The results were visualized using forest plots with 95% confidence intervals. Results were considered statistically significant in all analyses, when p-values were less than 0.05.
Feature selections - Venla
Correlation analysis between categorical variables was done by using Cramer’s V.

est plots with 95% confidence intervals. Results were considered statistically significant in all analyses, when p-values were less than 0.05.
Feature selections - Venla
Correlation analysis between categorical variables was done by using Cramer’s V. This provides standardized measures of association in the range of 0 (no association) to 1 (perfect association). Statistical significance was calculated using Pearson’s chi-square tests with significance level α = 0.05. 
For continuous variables we created a pipeline, which includes Variable Inflation Factor (VIF), LASSO and Random Forest to check the multicollinearity, reduce dimensionality and select the most predictive features. 
The VIF threshold is selected to be 5, so any variable that gives high multicollinearity (VIF > 5) is not removed. LASSO shrinks some coefficients to zero removing least important variables. LASSO uses cv.glmnet for cross-validation for the optimal lambda. Random Forest builds 1000 survival trees using the randomForestSRC package. The variables with importance score > 0 were selected from Random Forest. We combined the three variable selection methods and selected the variables that were selected at least in two of the three variable selection methods. 
Before adding these selected variables into the Cox model, we performed final feature selection, using recursive feature elimination. We used a repeated cross-validation C-index function and selected the feature combination to the Cox model, that gave the best model performance.

Whole Exome sequencing analysis & iMTB reports - for Andreas
Copy-number inference and genome instability quantification
Copy-number profiles were derived from segmented sequencing data using ASCAT, which provides allele-specific copy-number estimates together with tumour purity and ploidy inference (Van Loo et al., 2010). Given the high degree of aneuploidy in HGSC, copy-number alterations were defined using ploidy-normalised values, calculated as:
LogR =Lo
g

where 
CNtotal
 denotes the ASCAT-derived total copy number ( nAraw + nBraw), and 
ploidy
 corresponds to the tumour ploidy estimate for the respective sample. Segments with logR values greater than 1.3 were classified as amplifications, whereas segments with values below −1 were classified as deletions. The amplification threshold approximately corresponds to copy-number states exceeding ~2.5-fold above tumour ploidy and was selected to enrich for high-level focal amplification events. Conversely, the deletion threshold corresponds approximately to copy-number states below half the tumour ploidy and was intended to capture substantial allelic loss and deep deletion events. Segments that did not meet these criteria were considered copy-number neutral in downstream analyses.

For downstream analyses, samples were ranked according to FGA values and stratified into tertiles representing low, intermediate, and high levels of genomic alteration burden.
Whole-genome doubling (WGD) was inferred from allele-specific copy-number profiles using the framework described by Zhang et al. (2025), based on the fraction of the genome with major allele copy number ≥2 (FM2). For each sample, the fraction was calculated as:

 CONCLUSION
 • Spatial proteomics resolves HRD/HRP-associated immune TME states in HGSC.
 • Progression is associated with altered spatial immune architecture, marked by reduced immune-cell proximity to the tumor–
 stroma interface and distinct lymphoid–myeloid community structures, especially in HRP tumors.
 • Future work will integrate FGA and Xenium-defined niche programs to identify mechanisms linking genomic instability to
 spatial immune escape.

### word/document.xml
Responsible: Ziqi Kang
Timeline: 2024.08 - now
Description: 
SPACE means Precision oncology of spatial immune escape mechanisms in ovarian cancer. The aim is to uncover the spatial immune escape mechanisms in chemo-naive HGSC samples and profile the spatial landscape of the treatment-naive tumor microenvironment (TME). 
We will propose a special definition of niche in the tumor ecosystem - niche = physical niche + functional niche - and use the concept of “niche” as a tool to reveal immune escape mechanisms. Additionally, establishing a model to integrate multiomic datasets.
